== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Taku Shrine |画像 = [[File:Takushirine (4).jpg|280px]] |所在地 = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyōtango|Q513897}}{{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba}} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|38|09.8|N|135|04|45.2|E|region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=多久神社}} |祭神 = Toyouganome no Mikoto ({{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Ōkami}}) |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, former {{jalink|district shrine|Q134917316}} |創建 = (traditionally) 39th year of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, 7th month, 7th day |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} with corner bracket |別名 = Tenshu Daimyōjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Weekend in mid-October |神事 = {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto|label=多久神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taku Shrine''' (たくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|shrine|Q845945}} located in Ōaza Wakutayama, {{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba}}, {{jalink|Kyōtango|Q513897}}, {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}. Its main hall is registered as a {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}} {{jalink|tangible cultural property|Q2901860}} (building), and the {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632|Shibamukuri}} ritual performed at the autumn festival is registered as a Kyoto Prefecture {{jalink|intangible folk cultural property}}<ref name=郷土出版社>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=保存版 舞鶴・宮津・丹後の今昔 |publisher=郷土出版社 |date=2004 |page=55 |isbn=}}</ref>.
=== Enshrined deity === ; '''Toyouganome no Mikoto''' (とようがのめのみこと) : Also known as "{{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Ōkami}}"<ref name=丹後新風土記/>. According to tradition, Toyouganome no Mikoto is identified with the celestial maiden ({{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754|Hagoromo celestial maiden}}) of {{jalink|Mount Hiji|Q11126130}} mentioned in the lost passages of the ''{{jalink|Tango no Kuni Fudoki|Q11368517}}'', who failed to return to heaven and settled in this area, cultivating rice and creating a sake that could cure all ailments<ref name=デジタルF10>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.kyotango.lg.jp/top/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/3/1/1/3405.html |title=デジタルミュージアムF10多久神社本殿 |publisher =京丹後市 |accessdate=2020-09-21}}</ref>. For this reason, until the {{jalink|Meiji}} era, the shrine was also called "Tenshu Daimyōjin" (Heavenly Sake Great Deity)<ref name=丹後新風土記>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=丹後新風土記 |publisher=丹後広域観光キャンペーン協議会 |date=2008 |page=239 |isbn=}}</ref>. : Furthermore, there is a legend that sake once gushed from the foot of {{jalink|Wakutayama Tumulus Group|Q100549947|Wakutayama}} hill<ref name=丹後新風土記/>, and a folk song has been passed down: "From the Tenshu-sama of Yada and Tanba village, even the water flowing below is worthy of reverence"<ref name="峰山郷土史上,460p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=460 |isbn=}}</ref>.
=== History === === Overview history === The shrine is identified as the small shrine "Taku no Jinja" among the nine shrines of {{jalink|Tanba District}} listed in the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'' as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}<ref name="峰山郷土史454-455p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=454-455 |isbn=}}</ref>. According to tradition, the shrine was founded on the 7th day of the 7th month of the 39th year of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} at the site of a pond in {{jalink|Naka District (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11367236|Tanba Village}} where a {{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754|celestial maiden}} had descended<ref>{{Cite web |title=丹後史料叢書 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1175321/1/93?keyword=%E5%A4%9A%E4%B9%85%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2025-06-18 |publisher=国立国会図書館 |editor=丹後史料叢書刊行会 |year=1927 |language=ja |doi=10.11501/1175321}}</ref>. Before it was relocated to its current site during the {{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} era (1441-1444), it was located approximately 4,500 meters further to the west<ref name=京丹後デジタル/>.
The celestial maiden enshrined as the deity is identified with {{jalink|Toyouke Ōkami}}, and was revered as Tenshu Daimyōjin, the deity who brought sake brewing to this land. The founding site where the celestial maiden descended was a place called "Ota Pond" near where Taku Shrine stands in the early 21st century, and it is said that defiling it would bring a curse of heavy rain<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京都大事典府域編 |publisher=淡交社 |date=1994 |page=334 |isbn=}}</ref>. Notably, in the {{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754|Hagoromo tradition}} passed down in {{jalink|Mineyama}}, the pond where the celestial maiden descended is called "Manai" based on the lost passages of the ''{{jalink|Tango no Kuni Fudoki|Q11368517}}'' and the ''Tango Kyūjiki'', and the widely known theory holds that it is "Me-ike" (Female Pond) on {{jalink|Isanago-yama|Q31707112}}, which straddles {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}} and {{jalink|Ōmiya-chō (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11434439|Ōmiya-chō}}<ref name="#1">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=丹後みねやま羽衣伝説 |publisher=京都府峰山町 |date=1998年 |page=1 |isbn=}}</ref>
In documentary records, the ''Mineyama Meisaiki'' of 1753 (Hōreki 3) records that the shrine was "a six-shaku shrine dedicated to Tenshu Daimyōjin" and that "on the 8th of the 9th month, the miko Sagami of Kawabe Village was hired to perform the rites"<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=455 |isbn=}}</ref>.
The shrine served as the {{jalink|Tanba Village}} sōsha (head shrine) for the villages of Tanba, Naiki, Yada, Hashigi, Akasaka, Ishimaru, and Niiyama<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=会誌『旦波』第9号 |publisher=丹波の文化を伝承する会 |date=2016 |page=5 |isbn=}}</ref>. In 1873 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 6), it became the village shrine of {{jalink|Tanba Village|Q35797726}}<ref name="峰山郷土史上 459p"/>.
=== Taku Dispute === Around 1869 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 2), the Meiji government's policy of abolishing popular names and renaming shrines according to ancient {{jalink|Shinto|Q812767}} conventions triggered the "Taku Dispute" over the origins of '''Taku Shrine''' as the head shrine<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=457-458 |isbn=}}</ref>. A new theory was proposed: reading the two syllables of Tanba (丹波, ta-ni-ha) in reverse yields "ha-ni," and substituting the character for "two" (二) with "mi" gives "ha-mi," i.e., "Hami" (波弥), meaning the deity of food (hamu); therefore Tanba is the same as Hami, and the original site of Taku Shrine is a place in the neighboring villages of Arayama and Shinmachi called "Tako-dani" and "Tako-sengen," meaning the land of "Tako" -- that is, "Taku"<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p"/>. In response, Imanishi Iyo, who was then the custodian of Taku Shrine, petitioned the domain's shrine and temple office to clarify that {{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba||Tanba}} was unquestionably "Taniha" as recorded in ancient texts, and was emphatically not "Hami"<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p"/>.
The outcome of this dispute is not recorded in surviving documents, but according to the ''Jinja Torishirabechō'' (Shrine Investigation Record) submitted to the shrine and temple office, following the explanation of why Taku Shrine was relocated from its former site to its current location: "At that time (the early 1440s), Hashigi Village, Yada Village, Naiki Village, and Arayama Village had been conducting festival rites together, but due to a great dispute, the festival days became separate." This suggests that disputes with neighboring districts had existed for approximately 500 years prior<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p"/>.
=== Precincts === Behind the shrine stands the {{jalink|Wakutayama Tumulus Group|Q100549947}}, a group of 42 tumuli designated as a prefectural historic site, centering on a scallop-shaped {{jalink|keyhole-shaped burial mound|Q11268718}} approximately 100 meters in total length, along with various other large and small round tumuli. Behind the main hall, which is registered as a Kyoto Prefecture tangible cultural property and dates to the 17th century, stretches a forest of {{jalink|chinquapin|Q1065400}} and {{jalink|Mōsō bamboo|Q1330347}} trees. The {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632|Shibamukuri}} ritual folk performing art presented at the autumn festival is a prefecturally registered intangible folk cultural property that carries on the tradition of {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} fūryū kouta (elegant ballads), making this a region of great historical and cultural value<ref name=京丹後デジタル/>.
For this reason, on March 19, 2004 (Heisei 16), the main hall on the mountainside and approximately 1.7 {{jalink|hectares|Q35852}} of surrounding forest were designated as a prefectural Cultural Property Environmental Conservation District as "an area forming a valuable cultural landscape that should be preserved"<ref name=京丹後デジタル>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.kyotango.lg.jp/top/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/3/1/1/3458.html |title=多久神社文化財環境保全地区（府文化財環境保全地区） |publisher =京丹後市 |accessdate=2020-09-21}}</ref>.
=== Subsidiary shrines === In 1908 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 41), the following five shrines were merged as subsidiary shrines within the precincts<ref name=みねやまの文化財23p>{{Cite book|和書|author=峰山教育委員会 峰山町文化財保護審議会 |title=みねやまの文化財(社寺編) |publisher=峰山孔版社 |date=1981 |page=23 |isbn=}}</ref><ref name="峰山郷土史上,460p"/>. * Tatenui-sha - Enshrines Amawashi no Mikoto. * Yaegaki-sha - Enshrines Susanoo no Mikoto. * Hachiman-sha - Enshrines Homudawake no Mikoto. * Ōmiyagi-sha - Enshrines Ōmiyame no Mikoto. * Hiragaoka-sha - Enshrines Ukemochi no Ōkami. <gallery> File:多久神社 本殿正面.jpg|Worship hall File:多久神社 本殿内４.jpg|Main hall File:Takujinnja 社日の塔.jpg|Shajitsu-no-tō (shrine day tower) File:Takushirine (2).jpg|Torii gate and approach path File:Takushirine (3).jpg|Split worship hall-style building in front of the worship hall </gallery>
=== Shrine buildings === On September 25, 1811 ({{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka}} 8), the shrine suffered a fire that destroyed the main hall and its sheltering structure. The main hall that survives into the 21st century was rebuilt three years later using funds from the sale of five koku of shrine rice paddy land and donations from supporters<ref name=デジタルF10/>. The dance hall was also destroyed by fire in 1835 (Tenpō 6) and was rebuilt in 1877 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 10). The worship hall was newly constructed in 1912 ({{jalink|Taishō}} 1)<ref name="峰山郷土史上 459p"/>.
The shrine buildings surviving into the 21st century are as follows. ; Main hall <!--: It is believed to have been built between the mid-18th and early 19th centuries<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>。--><!--The explanation to the left contradicts the statement above that it was destroyed by fire in 1811.--> : The surviving main hall bears a {{jalink|giboshi ornament|Q3105399}} inscription dated Bunka 11, corresponding to 1814, and is believed to have been rebuilt by 1814 following the fire of 1811<ref name=デジタルF10/>. The building was the work of master carpenter Yoshioka Kahei, who was active in the {{jalink|Tango region}} at the time, and it survived without collapse even during the {{jalink|Kita-Tango earthquake|Q11400894}} of 1927 ({{jalink|Shōwa}} 2), which caused catastrophic damage throughout the {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}} area<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築/>. : It is a one-bay {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} main hall with corner brackets, a karahafu (cusped gable), and kokera-buki (wooden shingle) roofing, and is considered particularly valuable as a shrine main hall distinguished by its fine decorative carvings<ref name=デジタルF10/><ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築 |publisher=京丹後市 |date=2017 |page=465 |isbn=}}</ref>. A Kasuga-zukuri main hall is rare in the {{jalink|Tango region}}<ref name=丹後新風土記/>. : Although the main hall was severely damaged by the {{jalink|Kita-Tango earthquake|Q11400894}}, it was one of the few shrine buildings in {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}}, which suffered the greatest damage, to survive without collapse, and it was restored through approximately one month of repair work by the end of 1928 ({{jalink|Shōwa}} 3)<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築 |publisher=京丹後市 |date=2017 |page=272 |isbn=}}</ref><ref name="峰山郷土史上 459p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=459 |isbn=}}</ref>. ; Worship hall : An irimoya-zukuri (hipped gable) building, one bay in depth and two bays in width, with tsuma-iri (gable entry) and san-gawarabuki (pantile) roofing<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>. ; Sheltering structure : A kirizuma-zukuri (gable roof) building, four bays in depth and six bays in width, with tsuma-iri (gable entry) and steel sheet roofing<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>. ; Split worship hall-style building : A kirizuma-zukuri (gable roof) building, one bay in depth and one bay in width, with steel sheet roofing<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>.
=== Festivals === The main festivals are as follows. * New Year * Autumn Festival - The annual grand festival held on a weekend in mid-October, at which the traditional ritual {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}} is performed. However, in recent years, due to the declining birthrate, it is not held every year. * Minazuki Festival - Held on July 30 within the shrine precincts. Also called Kawasusosei (riverside festival)<ref name=みねやまの文化財23p/>.
=== Prefectural registered cultural properties === ; Main hall : See the "Shrine buildings" section for details. ; Tanba no Shibamukuri {{Wikisource|Tanba no Shibamukuri}} The ritual is performed on October 10 at the Taku Shrine festival. Because the call during the festival is "chaa," the popular name for the ritual became "chaa." In 1816 ({{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka}} 13), it was called "chau"<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=伊崎義明 |title=峰山郷土芸能集 |publisher=峰山よし出版 |date=1971 |page= |isbn=}}</ref>.
Four theories have been handed down regarding the origin of the "{{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632|Shibamukuri}} ritual"<ref name=丹波9,6p/>. The first relates to prayers for victory during the {{jalink|Mongol|Q3875368}} invasions<ref name=丹波9,6p/>. The second holds that it is a ritual based on ancient precedent in the ''{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}'', mimicking the scene of young men leading the way and purifying the path of evil spirits with wasako bamboo ({{jalink|bamboo grass}})<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=子供がつづる丹後の歴史 |publisher=黒川美富子 |date=1980 |page=169 |isbn=}}</ref>. The third theory traces its origin to {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} {{jalink|dengaku|Q3022455}}, and the fourth attributes it to the "Sasa-bayashi" tradition found throughout the {{jalink|Tango region}}<ref name=丹波9,6p>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=会誌『旦波』第九号 |publisher=丹波の文化を伝承する会 |date=2016 |page=6 |isbn=}}</ref>.
=== Related sites === * {{jalink|Isanago-yama|Q31707112}} - A {{jalink|sacred mountain}} known for the Mount Hiji legend ({{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754}}) handed down in {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}}. It is said to be the site where "the celestial maiden of Hiji" descended, bringing rice cultivation and sake brewing to the {{jalink|Tango region}}<ref name="#1"/>.
=== Local information === === Location === * Address - {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyōtango|Q513897}}{{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}}{{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba||Tanba}} Wakutayama 2<ref name=アクセス/> * The shrine is located 1,262 meters northeast of {{jalink|Mineyama Station|Q6864579}} in a straight line, at an elevation of 29 meters above sea level<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mapion.co.jp/phonebook/M06005/26212/ILSP0061180050_ipclm/ |title=多久神社 |publisher =Mapion電話帳 |accessdate=2020-09-18}}</ref>. * Transportation access ** Railway - {{jalink|Kyoto Tango Railway}} "{{jalink|Mineyama Station|Q6864579}}" - 15 minutes on foot ** Bus - {{jalink|Tankai Bus}} "Tanba-guchi" bus stop - 3 minutes on foot; "Yada-bashi" bus stop - 6 minutes on foot <ref name=アクセス>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://yaokami.jp/1261189/ |title=多久神社アクセス |publisher =八百万の神 |accessdate=2020-09-21}}</ref>
=== Surroundings === * {{jalink|Wakutayama Tumulus Group|Q100549947}} - An extensive tumulus group numbering 42 mounds in total, located on the hills behind Taku Shrine<ref name=京丹後市の考古資料132p>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京丹後市の考古資料 |publisher=京丹後市 |date=2010 |page=132 |isbn=}}</ref>. * {{jalink|Kyōtango Municipal Tanba Elementary School|Q100594759|Former Tanba Elementary School}} - An elementary school located in the center of the {{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba}} district, founded in 1872 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 5) and closed in March 2018 (Heisei 30)<ref>「京丹後市 18年度、丹波と新山小を統合 19年度は豊栄と間人も」『毎日新聞』2016年8月30日</ref>. The students of this school performed the traditional ritual "{{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}}" at the annual grand festival<ref name=京都新聞19970509>{{Cite news |title=まちの文化財６ |newspaper=京都新聞 |date=1997-05-09 |author= |page=}}</ref>.
=== Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} <!-- === Annotations === {{notelist}} --> === Sources === {{reflist|2}}
=== References === * ''Mineyama Kyōdoshi, Volume 1'' (峰山郷土史 上, Mineyama Local History, Volume 1), Mineyama-chō, 1963 * ''Kyōtango-shi no Machinami, Kenchiku'' (京丹後市のまちなみ・建築, Streetscapes and Architecture of Kyōtango City), Kyōtango City, 2017 * Mineyama Board of Education, Mineyama-chō Cultural Properties Protection Council, ''Mineyama no Bunkazai (Shaji-hen)'' (みねやまの文化財(社寺編), Cultural Properties of Mineyama (Shrines and Temples Edition)), Mineyama Kōhansha, 1981 * ''Hozonban Maizuru, Miyazu, Tango no Konjaku'' (保存版 舞鶴・宮津・丹後の今昔, Preservation Edition: Maizuru, Miyazu, and Tango, Past and Present), Kyōdo Shuppansha, 2004 * ''Tango Shin Fudoki'' (丹後新風土記, New Tango Fudoki), Tango Wide-Area Tourism Campaign Council, 2008 * ''Kaishi "Tanba" Dai-9-gō'' (会誌『但波』第9号, Journal "Tanba" No. 9), Association for Transmitting the Culture of Tanba, 2016 * ''Kyōto Daijiten Fuiki-hen'' (京都大事典府域編, Great Encyclopedia of Kyoto, Prefectural Area Edition), {{jalink|Tankōsha|Q11559760}}, 1994 * ''Tango Mineyama Hagoromo Densetsu'' (丹後みねやま羽衣伝説, Tango Mineyama Hagoromo Legend), Kyoto Prefecture Mineyama-chō, 1998 * ''Tango Shiryō Sōsho'' (丹後史料叢書, Tango Historical Documents Collection), Volume 1, Tango Shiryō Sōsho Kankōkai, 1927. National Diet Library Digital Collection <nowiki>https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1175321</nowiki> (accessed 2025-06-18)
=== Related articles === * {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}} * {{jalink|Tango Tōji|Q109597465}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Taku Shrine | native_name = 多久神社 | image = Takushirine (4).jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Taku Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35.635451|135.079379}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }}
{{nihongo|'''Taku Shrine'''|多久神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Tango Province]].

[[JA:多久神社]]
{{wikidata link|Q100551246}}
== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Taku Shrine |画像 = [[File:Takushirine (4).jpg|280px]] |所在地 = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyōtango|Q513897}}{{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba}} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|38|09.8|N|135|04|45.2|E|region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=多久神社}} |祭神 = Toyouganome no Mikoto ({{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Ōkami}}) |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, former {{jalink|district shrine|Q134917316}} |創建 = (traditionally) 39th year of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, 7th month, 7th day |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} with corner bracket |別名 = Tenshu Daimyōjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Weekend in mid-October |神事 = {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto|label=多久神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taku Shrine''' (たくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|shrine|Q845945}} located in Ōaza Wakutayama, {{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba}}, {{jalink|Kyōtango|Q513897}}, {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}. Its main hall is registered as a {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}} {{jalink|tangible cultural property|Q2901860}} (building), and the {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632|Shibamukuri}} ritual performed at the autumn festival is registered as a Kyoto Prefecture {{jalink|intangible folk cultural property}}<ref name=郷土出版社>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=保存版 舞鶴・宮津・丹後の今昔 |publisher=郷土出版社 |date=2004 |page=55 |isbn=}}</ref>.
=== Enshrined deity === ; '''Toyouganome no Mikoto''' (とようがのめのみこと) : Also known as "{{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Ōkami}}"<ref name=丹後新風土記/>. According to tradition, Toyouganome no Mikoto is identified with the celestial maiden ({{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754|Hagoromo celestial maiden}}) of {{jalink|Mount Hiji|Q11126130}} mentioned in the lost passages of the ''{{jalink|Tango no Kuni Fudoki|Q11368517}}'', who failed to return to heaven and settled in this area, cultivating rice and creating a sake that could cure all ailments<ref name=デジタルF10>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.kyotango.lg.jp/top/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/3/1/1/3405.html |title=デジタルミュージアムF10多久神社本殿 |publisher =京丹後市 |accessdate=2020-09-21}}</ref>. For this reason, until the {{jalink|Meiji}} era, the shrine was also called "Tenshu Daimyōjin" (Heavenly Sake Great Deity)<ref name=丹後新風土記>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=丹後新風土記 |publisher=丹後広域観光キャンペーン協議会 |date=2008 |page=239 |isbn=}}</ref>. : Furthermore, there is a legend that sake once gushed from the foot of {{jalink|Wakutayama Tumulus Group|Q100549947|Wakutayama}} hill<ref name=丹後新風土記/>, and a folk song has been passed down: "From the Tenshu-sama of Yada and Tanba village, even the water flowing below is worthy of reverence"<ref name="峰山郷土史上,460p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=460 |isbn=}}</ref>.
=== History === === Overview history === The shrine is identified as the small shrine "Taku no Jinja" among the nine shrines of {{jalink|Tanba District}} listed in the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'' as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}<ref name="峰山郷土史454-455p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=454-455 |isbn=}}</ref>. According to tradition, the shrine was founded on the 7th day of the 7th month of the 39th year of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} at the site of a pond in {{jalink|Naka District (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11367236|Tanba Village}} where a {{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754|celestial maiden}} had descended<ref>{{Cite web |title=丹後史料叢書 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1175321/1/93?keyword=%E5%A4%9A%E4%B9%85%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2025-06-18 |publisher=国立国会図書館 |editor=丹後史料叢書刊行会 |year=1927 |language=ja |doi=10.11501/1175321}}</ref>. Before it was relocated to its current site during the {{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} era (1441-1444), it was located approximately 4,500 meters further to the west<ref name=京丹後デジタル/>.
The celestial maiden enshrined as the deity is identified with {{jalink|Toyouke Ōkami}}, and was revered as Tenshu Daimyōjin, the deity who brought sake brewing to this land. The founding site where the celestial maiden descended was a place called "Ota Pond" near where Taku Shrine stands in the early 21st century, and it is said that defiling it would bring a curse of heavy rain<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京都大事典府域編 |publisher=淡交社 |date=1994 |page=334 |isbn=}}</ref>. Notably, in the {{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754|Hagoromo tradition}} passed down in {{jalink|Mineyama}}, the pond where the celestial maiden descended is called "Manai" based on the lost passages of the ''{{jalink|Tango no Kuni Fudoki|Q11368517}}'' and the ''Tango Kyūjiki'', and the widely known theory holds that it is "Me-ike" (Female Pond) on {{jalink|Isanago-yama|Q31707112}}, which straddles {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}} and {{jalink|Ōmiya-chō (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11434439|Ōmiya-chō}}<ref name="#1">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=丹後みねやま羽衣伝説 |publisher=京都府峰山町 |date=1998年 |page=1 |isbn=}}</ref>
In documentary records, the ''Mineyama Meisaiki'' of 1753 (Hōreki 3) records that the shrine was "a six-shaku shrine dedicated to Tenshu Daimyōjin" and that "on the 8th of the 9th month, the miko Sagami of Kawabe Village was hired to perform the rites"<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=455 |isbn=}}</ref>.
The shrine served as the {{jalink|Tanba Village}} sōsha (head shrine) for the villages of Tanba, Naiki, Yada, Hashigi, Akasaka, Ishimaru, and Niiyama<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=会誌『旦波』第9号 |publisher=丹波の文化を伝承する会 |date=2016 |page=5 |isbn=}}</ref>. In 1873 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 6), it became the village shrine of {{jalink|Tanba Village|Q35797726}}<ref name="峰山郷土史上 459p"/>.
=== Taku Dispute === Around 1869 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 2), the Meiji government's policy of abolishing popular names and renaming shrines according to ancient {{jalink|Shinto|Q812767}} conventions triggered the "Taku Dispute" over the origins of '''Taku Shrine''' as the head shrine<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=457-458 |isbn=}}</ref>. A new theory was proposed: reading the two syllables of Tanba (丹波, ta-ni-ha) in reverse yields "ha-ni," and substituting the character for "two" (二) with "mi" gives "ha-mi," i.e., "Hami" (波弥), meaning the deity of food (hamu); therefore Tanba is the same as Hami, and the original site of Taku Shrine is a place in the neighboring villages of Arayama and Shinmachi called "Tako-dani" and "Tako-sengen," meaning the land of "Tako" -- that is, "Taku"<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p"/>. In response, Imanishi Iyo, who was then the custodian of Taku Shrine, petitioned the domain's shrine and temple office to clarify that {{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba||Tanba}} was unquestionably "Taniha" as recorded in ancient texts, and was emphatically not "Hami"<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p"/>.
The outcome of this dispute is not recorded in surviving documents, but according to the ''Jinja Torishirabechō'' (Shrine Investigation Record) submitted to the shrine and temple office, following the explanation of why Taku Shrine was relocated from its former site to its current location: "At that time (the early 1440s), Hashigi Village, Yada Village, Naiki Village, and Arayama Village had been conducting festival rites together, but due to a great dispute, the festival days became separate." This suggests that disputes with neighboring districts had existed for approximately 500 years prior<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p"/>.
=== Precincts === Behind the shrine stands the {{jalink|Wakutayama Tumulus Group|Q100549947}}, a group of 42 tumuli designated as a prefectural historic site, centering on a scallop-shaped {{jalink|keyhole-shaped burial mound|Q11268718}} approximately 100 meters in total length, along with various other large and small round tumuli. Behind the main hall, which is registered as a Kyoto Prefecture tangible cultural property and dates to the 17th century, stretches a forest of {{jalink|chinquapin|Q1065400}} and {{jalink|Mōsō bamboo|Q1330347}} trees. The {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632|Shibamukuri}} ritual folk performing art presented at the autumn festival is a prefecturally registered intangible folk cultural property that carries on the tradition of {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} fūryū kouta (elegant ballads), making this a region of great historical and cultural value<ref name=京丹後デジタル/>.
For this reason, on March 19, 2004 (Heisei 16), the main hall on the mountainside and approximately 1.7 {{jalink|hectares|Q35852}} of surrounding forest were designated as a prefectural Cultural Property Environmental Conservation District as "an area forming a valuable cultural landscape that should be preserved"<ref name=京丹後デジタル>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.kyotango.lg.jp/top/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/3/1/1/3458.html |title=多久神社文化財環境保全地区（府文化財環境保全地区） |publisher =京丹後市 |accessdate=2020-09-21}}</ref>.
=== Subsidiary shrines === In 1908 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 41), the following five shrines were merged as subsidiary shrines within the precincts<ref name=みねやまの文化財23p>{{Cite book|和書|author=峰山教育委員会 峰山町文化財保護審議会 |title=みねやまの文化財(社寺編) |publisher=峰山孔版社 |date=1981 |page=23 |isbn=}}</ref><ref name="峰山郷土史上,460p"/>. * Tatenui-sha - Enshrines Amawashi no Mikoto. * Yaegaki-sha - Enshrines Susanoo no Mikoto. * Hachiman-sha - Enshrines Homudawake no Mikoto. * Ōmiyagi-sha - Enshrines Ōmiyame no Mikoto. * Hiragaoka-sha - Enshrines Ukemochi no Ōkami. <gallery> File:多久神社 本殿正面.jpg|Worship hall File:多久神社 本殿内４.jpg|Main hall File:Takujinnja 社日の塔.jpg|Shajitsu-no-tō (shrine day tower) File:Takushirine (2).jpg|Torii gate and approach path File:Takushirine (3).jpg|Split worship hall-style building in front of the worship hall </gallery>
=== Shrine buildings === On September 25, 1811 ({{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka}} 8), the shrine suffered a fire that destroyed the main hall and its sheltering structure. The main hall that survives into the 21st century was rebuilt three years later using funds from the sale of five koku of shrine rice paddy land and donations from supporters<ref name=デジタルF10/>. The dance hall was also destroyed by fire in 1835 (Tenpō 6) and was rebuilt in 1877 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 10). The worship hall was newly constructed in 1912 ({{jalink|Taishō}} 1)<ref name="峰山郷土史上 459p"/>.
The shrine buildings surviving into the 21st century are as follows. ; Main hall <!--: It is believed to have been built between the mid-18th and early 19th centuries<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>。--><!--The explanation to the left contradicts the statement above that it was destroyed by fire in 1811.--> : The surviving main hall bears a {{jalink|giboshi ornament|Q3105399}} inscription dated Bunka 11, corresponding to 1814, and is believed to have been rebuilt by 1814 following the fire of 1811<ref name=デジタルF10/>. The building was the work of master carpenter Yoshioka Kahei, who was active in the {{jalink|Tango region}} at the time, and it survived without collapse even during the {{jalink|Kita-Tango earthquake|Q11400894}} of 1927 ({{jalink|Shōwa}} 2), which caused catastrophic damage throughout the {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}} area<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築/>. : It is a one-bay {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} main hall with corner brackets, a karahafu (cusped gable), and kokera-buki (wooden shingle) roofing, and is considered particularly valuable as a shrine main hall distinguished by its fine decorative carvings<ref name=デジタルF10/><ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築 |publisher=京丹後市 |date=2017 |page=465 |isbn=}}</ref>. A Kasuga-zukuri main hall is rare in the {{jalink|Tango region}}<ref name=丹後新風土記/>. : Although the main hall was severely damaged by the {{jalink|Kita-Tango earthquake|Q11400894}}, it was one of the few shrine buildings in {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}}, which suffered the greatest damage, to survive without collapse, and it was restored through approximately one month of repair work by the end of 1928 ({{jalink|Shōwa}} 3)<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築 |publisher=京丹後市 |date=2017 |page=272 |isbn=}}</ref><ref name="峰山郷土史上 459p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=459 |isbn=}}</ref>. ; Worship hall : An irimoya-zukuri (hipped gable) building, one bay in depth and two bays in width, with tsuma-iri (gable entry) and san-gawarabuki (pantile) roofing<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>. ; Sheltering structure : A kirizuma-zukuri (gable roof) building, four bays in depth and six bays in width, with tsuma-iri (gable entry) and steel sheet roofing<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>. ; Split worship hall-style building : A kirizuma-zukuri (gable roof) building, one bay in depth and one bay in width, with steel sheet roofing<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>.
=== Festivals === The main festivals are as follows. * New Year * Autumn Festival - The annual grand festival held on a weekend in mid-October, at which the traditional ritual {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}} is performed. However, in recent years, due to the declining birthrate, it is not held every year. * Minazuki Festival - Held on July 30 within the shrine precincts. Also called Kawasusosei (riverside festival)<ref name=みねやまの文化財23p/>.
=== Prefectural registered cultural properties === ; Main hall : See the "Shrine buildings" section for details. ; Tanba no Shibamukuri {{Wikisource|Tanba no Shibamukuri}} The ritual is performed on October 10 at the Taku Shrine festival. Because the call during the festival is "chaa," the popular name for the ritual became "chaa." In 1816 ({{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka}} 13), it was called "chau"<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=伊崎義明 |title=峰山郷土芸能集 |publisher=峰山よし出版 |date=1971 |page= |isbn=}}</ref>.
Four theories have been handed down regarding the origin of the "{{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632|Shibamukuri}} ritual"<ref name=丹波9,6p/>. The first relates to prayers for victory during the {{jalink|Mongol|Q3875368}} invasions<ref name=丹波9,6p/>. The second holds that it is a ritual based on ancient precedent in the ''{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}'', mimicking the scene of young men leading the way and purifying the path of evil spirits with wasako bamboo ({{jalink|bamboo grass}})<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=子供がつづる丹後の歴史 |publisher=黒川美富子 |date=1980 |page=169 |isbn=}}</ref>. The third theory traces its origin to {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} {{jalink|dengaku|Q3022455}}, and the fourth attributes it to the "Sasa-bayashi" tradition found throughout the {{jalink|Tango region}}<ref name=丹波9,6p>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=会誌『旦波』第九号 |publisher=丹波の文化を伝承する会 |date=2016 |page=6 |isbn=}}</ref>.
=== Related sites === * {{jalink|Isanago-yama|Q31707112}} - A {{jalink|sacred mountain}} known for the Mount Hiji legend ({{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754}}) handed down in {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}}. It is said to be the site where "the celestial maiden of Hiji" descended, bringing rice cultivation and sake brewing to the {{jalink|Tango region}}<ref name="#1"/>.
=== Local information === === Location === * Address - {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyōtango|Q513897}}{{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}}{{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba||Tanba}} Wakutayama 2<ref name=アクセス/> * The shrine is located 1,262 meters northeast of {{jalink|Mineyama Station|Q6864579}} in a straight line, at an elevation of 29 meters above sea level<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mapion.co.jp/phonebook/M06005/26212/ILSP0061180050_ipclm/ |title=多久神社 |publisher =Mapion電話帳 |accessdate=2020-09-18}}</ref>. * Transportation access ** Railway - {{jalink|Kyoto Tango Railway}} "{{jalink|Mineyama Station|Q6864579}}" - 15 minutes on foot ** Bus - {{jalink|Tankai Bus}} "Tanba-guchi" bus stop - 3 minutes on foot; "Yada-bashi" bus stop - 6 minutes on foot <ref name=アクセス>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://yaokami.jp/1261189/ |title=多久神社アクセス |publisher =八百万の神 |accessdate=2020-09-21}}</ref>
=== Surroundings === * {{jalink|Wakutayama Tumulus Group|Q100549947}} - An extensive tumulus group numbering 42 mounds in total, located on the hills behind Taku Shrine<ref name=京丹後市の考古資料132p>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京丹後市の考古資料 |publisher=京丹後市 |date=2010 |page=132 |isbn=}}</ref>. * {{jalink|Kyōtango Municipal Tanba Elementary School|Q100594759|Former Tanba Elementary School}} - An elementary school located in the center of the {{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba}} district, founded in 1872 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 5) and closed in March 2018 (Heisei 30)<ref>「京丹後市 18年度、丹波と新山小を統合 19年度は豊栄と間人も」『毎日新聞』2016年8月30日</ref>. The students of this school performed the traditional ritual "{{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}}" at the annual grand festival<ref name=京都新聞19970509>{{Cite news |title=まちの文化財６ |newspaper=京都新聞 |date=1997-05-09 |author= |page=}}</ref>.
=== Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} <!-- === Annotations === {{notelist}} --> === Sources === {{reflist|2}}
=== References === * ''Mineyama Kyōdoshi, Volume 1'' (峰山郷土史 上, Mineyama Local History, Volume 1), Mineyama-chō, 1963 * ''Kyōtango-shi no Machinami, Kenchiku'' (京丹後市のまちなみ・建築, Streetscapes and Architecture of Kyōtango City), Kyōtango City, 2017 * Mineyama Board of Education, Mineyama-chō Cultural Properties Protection Council, ''Mineyama no Bunkazai (Shaji-hen)'' (みねやまの文化財(社寺編), Cultural Properties of Mineyama (Shrines and Temples Edition)), Mineyama Kōhansha, 1981 * ''Hozonban Maizuru, Miyazu, Tango no Konjaku'' (保存版 舞鶴・宮津・丹後の今昔, Preservation Edition: Maizuru, Miyazu, and Tango, Past and Present), Kyōdo Shuppansha, 2004 * ''Tango Shin Fudoki'' (丹後新風土記, New Tango Fudoki), Tango Wide-Area Tourism Campaign Council, 2008 * ''Kaishi "Tanba" Dai-9-gō'' (会誌『但波』第9号, Journal "Tanba" No. 9), Association for Transmitting the Culture of Tanba, 2016 * ''Kyōto Daijiten Fuiki-hen'' (京都大事典府域編, Great Encyclopedia of Kyoto, Prefectural Area Edition), {{jalink|Tankōsha|Q11559760}}, 1994 * ''Tango Mineyama Hagoromo Densetsu'' (丹後みねやま羽衣伝説, Tango Mineyama Hagoromo Legend), Kyoto Prefecture Mineyama-chō, 1998 * ''Tango Shiryō Sōsho'' (丹後史料叢書, Tango Historical Documents Collection), Volume 1, Tango Shiryō Sōsho Kankōkai, 1927. National Diet Library Digital Collection <nowiki>https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1175321</nowiki> (accessed 2025-06-18)

{{Infobox religious building | name = Taku Shrine | native_name = 多久神社 | image = Takushirine (4).jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Taku Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35.635451|135.079379}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }}
{{nihongo|'''Taku Shrine'''|多久神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Tango Province]].

[[JA:多久神社]]
{{wikidata link|Q100551246}}
== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Taku Shrine |画像 = [[File:Takushirine (4).jpg|280px]] |所在地 = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyōtango|Q513897}}{{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba}} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|38|09.8|N|135|04|45.2|E|region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=多久神社}} |祭神 = Toyouganome no Mikoto ({{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Ōkami}}) |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, former {{jalink|district shrine|Q134917316}} |創建 = (traditionally) 39th year of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, 7th month, 7th day |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} with corner bracket |別名 = Tenshu Daimyōjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Weekend in mid-October |神事 = {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto|label=多久神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taku Shrine''' (たくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|shrine|Q845945}} located in Ōaza Wakutayama, {{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba}}, {{jalink|Kyōtango|Q513897}}, {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}. Its main hall is registered as a {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}} {{jalink|tangible cultural property|Q2901860}} (building), and the {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632|Shibamukuri}} ritual performed at the autumn festival is registered as a Kyoto Prefecture {{jalink|intangible folk cultural property}}<ref name=郷土出版社>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=保存版 舞鶴・宮津・丹後の今昔 |publisher=郷土出版社 |date=2004 |page=55 |isbn=}}</ref>.
=== Enshrined deity === ; '''Toyouganome no Mikoto''' (とようがのめのみこと) : Also known as "{{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Ōkami}}"<ref name=丹後新風土記/>. According to tradition, Toyouganome no Mikoto is identified with the celestial maiden ({{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754|Hagoromo celestial maiden}}) of {{jalink|Mount Hiji|Q11126130}} mentioned in the lost passages of the ''{{jalink|Tango no Kuni Fudoki|Q11368517}}'', who failed to return to heaven and settled in this area, cultivating rice and creating a sake that could cure all ailments<ref name=デジタルF10>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.kyotango.lg.jp/top/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/3/1/1/3405.html |title=デジタルミュージアムF10多久神社本殿 |publisher =京丹後市 |accessdate=2020-09-21}}</ref>. For this reason, until the {{jalink|Meiji}} era, the shrine was also called "Tenshu Daimyōjin" (Heavenly Sake Great Deity)<ref name=丹後新風土記>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=丹後新風土記 |publisher=丹後広域観光キャンペーン協議会 |date=2008 |page=239 |isbn=}}</ref>. : Furthermore, there is a legend that sake once gushed from the foot of {{jalink|Wakutayama Tumulus Group|Q100549947|Wakutayama}} hill<ref name=丹後新風土記/>, and a folk song has been passed down: "From the Tenshu-sama of Yada and Tanba village, even the water flowing below is worthy of reverence"<ref name="峰山郷土史上,460p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=460 |isbn=}}</ref>.
=== History === === Overview history === The shrine is identified as the small shrine "Taku no Jinja" among the nine shrines of {{jalink|Tanba District}} listed in the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'' as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}<ref name="峰山郷土史454-455p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=454-455 |isbn=}}</ref>. According to tradition, the shrine was founded on the 7th day of the 7th month of the 39th year of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} at the site of a pond in {{jalink|Naka District (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11367236|Tanba Village}} where a {{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754|celestial maiden}} had descended<ref>{{Cite web |title=丹後史料叢書 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1175321/1/93?keyword=%E5%A4%9A%E4%B9%85%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2025-06-18 |publisher=国立国会図書館 |editor=丹後史料叢書刊行会 |year=1927 |language=ja |doi=10.11501/1175321}}</ref>. Before it was relocated to its current site during the {{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} era (1441-1444), it was located approximately 4,500 meters further to the west<ref name=京丹後デジタル/>.
The celestial maiden enshrined as the deity is identified with {{jalink|Toyouke Ōkami}}, and was revered as Tenshu Daimyōjin, the deity who brought sake brewing to this land. The founding site where the celestial maiden descended was a place called "Ota Pond" near where Taku Shrine stands in the early 21st century, and it is said that defiling it would bring a curse of heavy rain<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京都大事典府域編 |publisher=淡交社 |date=1994 |page=334 |isbn=}}</ref>. Notably, in the {{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754|Hagoromo tradition}} passed down in {{jalink|Mineyama}}, the pond where the celestial maiden descended is called "Manai" based on the lost passages of the ''{{jalink|Tango no Kuni Fudoki|Q11368517}}'' and the ''Tango Kyūjiki'', and the widely known theory holds that it is "Me-ike" (Female Pond) on {{jalink|Isanago-yama|Q31707112}}, which straddles {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}} and {{jalink|Ōmiya-chō (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11434439|Ōmiya-chō}}<ref name="#1">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=丹後みねやま羽衣伝説 |publisher=京都府峰山町 |date=1998年 |page=1 |isbn=}}</ref>
In documentary records, the ''Mineyama Meisaiki'' of 1753 (Hōreki 3) records that the shrine was "a six-shaku shrine dedicated to Tenshu Daimyōjin" and that "on the 8th of the 9th month, the miko Sagami of Kawabe Village was hired to perform the rites"<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=455 |isbn=}}</ref>.
The shrine served as the {{jalink|Tanba Village}} sōsha (head shrine) for the villages of Tanba, Naiki, Yada, Hashigi, Akasaka, Ishimaru, and Niiyama<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=会誌『旦波』第9号 |publisher=丹波の文化を伝承する会 |date=2016 |page=5 |isbn=}}</ref>. In 1873 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 6), it became the village shrine of {{jalink|Tanba Village|Q35797726}}<ref name="峰山郷土史上 459p"/>.
=== Taku Dispute === Around 1869 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 2), the Meiji government's policy of abolishing popular names and renaming shrines according to ancient {{jalink|Shinto|Q812767}} conventions triggered the "Taku Dispute" over the origins of '''Taku Shrine''' as the head shrine<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=457-458 |isbn=}}</ref>. A new theory was proposed: reading the two syllables of Tanba (丹波, ta-ni-ha) in reverse yields "ha-ni," and substituting the character for "two" (二) with "mi" gives "ha-mi," i.e., "Hami" (波弥), meaning the deity of food (hamu); therefore Tanba is the same as Hami, and the original site of Taku Shrine is a place in the neighboring villages of Arayama and Shinmachi called "Tako-dani" and "Tako-sengen," meaning the land of "Tako" -- that is, "Taku"<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p"/>. In response, Imanishi Iyo, who was then the custodian of Taku Shrine, petitioned the domain's shrine and temple office to clarify that {{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba||Tanba}} was unquestionably "Taniha" as recorded in ancient texts, and was emphatically not "Hami"<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p"/>.
The outcome of this dispute is not recorded in surviving documents, but according to the ''Jinja Torishirabechō'' (Shrine Investigation Record) submitted to the shrine and temple office, following the explanation of why Taku Shrine was relocated from its former site to its current location: "At that time (the early 1440s), Hashigi Village, Yada Village, Naiki Village, and Arayama Village had been conducting festival rites together, but due to a great dispute, the festival days became separate." This suggests that disputes with neighboring districts had existed for approximately 500 years prior<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p"/>.
=== Precincts === Behind the shrine stands the {{jalink|Wakutayama Tumulus Group|Q100549947}}, a group of 42 tumuli designated as a prefectural historic site, centering on a scallop-shaped {{jalink|keyhole-shaped burial mound|Q11268718}} approximately 100 meters in total length, along with various other large and small round tumuli. Behind the main hall, which is registered as a Kyoto Prefecture tangible cultural property and dates to the 17th century, stretches a forest of {{jalink|chinquapin|Q1065400}} and {{jalink|Mōsō bamboo|Q1330347}} trees. The {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632|Shibamukuri}} ritual folk performing art presented at the autumn festival is a prefecturally registered intangible folk cultural property that carries on the tradition of {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} fūryū kouta (elegant ballads), making this a region of great historical and cultural value<ref name=京丹後デジタル/>.
For this reason, on March 19, 2004 (Heisei 16), the main hall on the mountainside and approximately 1.7 {{jalink|hectares|Q35852}} of surrounding forest were designated as a prefectural Cultural Property Environmental Conservation District as "an area forming a valuable cultural landscape that should be preserved"<ref name=京丹後デジタル>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.kyotango.lg.jp/top/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/3/1/1/3458.html |title=多久神社文化財環境保全地区（府文化財環境保全地区） |publisher =京丹後市 |accessdate=2020-09-21}}</ref>.
=== Subsidiary shrines === In 1908 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 41), the following five shrines were merged as subsidiary shrines within the precincts<ref name=みねやまの文化財23p>{{Cite book|和書|author=峰山教育委員会 峰山町文化財保護審議会 |title=みねやまの文化財(社寺編) |publisher=峰山孔版社 |date=1981 |page=23 |isbn=}}</ref><ref name="峰山郷土史上,460p"/>. * Tatenui-sha - Enshrines Amawashi no Mikoto. * Yaegaki-sha - Enshrines Susanoo no Mikoto. * Hachiman-sha - Enshrines Homudawake no Mikoto. * Ōmiyagi-sha - Enshrines Ōmiyame no Mikoto. * Hiragaoka-sha - Enshrines Ukemochi no Ōkami. <gallery> File:多久神社 本殿正面.jpg|Worship hall File:多久神社 本殿内４.jpg|Main hall File:Takujinnja 社日の塔.jpg|Shajitsu-no-tō (shrine day tower) File:Takushirine (2).jpg|Torii gate and approach path File:Takushirine (3).jpg|Split worship hall-style building in front of the worship hall </gallery>
=== Shrine buildings === On September 25, 1811 ({{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka}} 8), the shrine suffered a fire that destroyed the main hall and its sheltering structure. The main hall that survives into the 21st century was rebuilt three years later using funds from the sale of five koku of shrine rice paddy land and donations from supporters<ref name=デジタルF10/>. The dance hall was also destroyed by fire in 1835 (Tenpō 6) and was rebuilt in 1877 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 10). The worship hall was newly constructed in 1912 ({{jalink|Taishō}} 1)<ref name="峰山郷土史上 459p"/>.
The shrine buildings surviving into the 21st century are as follows. ; Main hall <!--: It is believed to have been built between the mid-18th and early 19th centuries<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>。--><!--The explanation to the left contradicts the statement above that it was destroyed by fire in 1811.--> : The surviving main hall bears a {{jalink|giboshi ornament|Q3105399}} inscription dated Bunka 11, corresponding to 1814, and is believed to have been rebuilt by 1814 following the fire of 1811<ref name=デジタルF10/>. The building was the work of master carpenter Yoshioka Kahei, who was active in the {{jalink|Tango region}} at the time, and it survived without collapse even during the {{jalink|Kita-Tango earthquake|Q11400894}} of 1927 ({{jalink|Shōwa}} 2), which caused catastrophic damage throughout the {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}} area<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築/>. : It is a one-bay {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} main hall with corner brackets, a karahafu (cusped gable), and kokera-buki (wooden shingle) roofing, and is considered particularly valuable as a shrine main hall distinguished by its fine decorative carvings<ref name=デジタルF10/><ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築 |publisher=京丹後市 |date=2017 |page=465 |isbn=}}</ref>. A Kasuga-zukuri main hall is rare in the {{jalink|Tango region}}<ref name=丹後新風土記/>. : Although the main hall was severely damaged by the {{jalink|Kita-Tango earthquake|Q11400894}}, it was one of the few shrine buildings in {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}}, which suffered the greatest damage, to survive without collapse, and it was restored through approximately one month of repair work by the end of 1928 ({{jalink|Shōwa}} 3)<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築 |publisher=京丹後市 |date=2017 |page=272 |isbn=}}</ref><ref name="峰山郷土史上 459p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=459 |isbn=}}</ref>. ; Worship hall : An irimoya-zukuri (hipped gable) building, one bay in depth and two bays in width, with tsuma-iri (gable entry) and san-gawarabuki (pantile) roofing<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>. ; Sheltering structure : A kirizuma-zukuri (gable roof) building, four bays in depth and six bays in width, with tsuma-iri (gable entry) and steel sheet roofing<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>. ; Split worship hall-style building : A kirizuma-zukuri (gable roof) building, one bay in depth and one bay in width, with steel sheet roofing<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>.
=== Festivals === The main festivals are as follows. * New Year * Autumn Festival - The annual grand festival held on a weekend in mid-October, at which the traditional ritual {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}} is performed. However, in recent years, due to the declining birthrate, it is not held every year. * Minazuki Festival - Held on July 30 within the shrine precincts. Also called Kawasusosei (riverside festival)<ref name=みねやまの文化財23p/>.
=== Prefectural registered cultural properties === ; Main hall : See the "Shrine buildings" section for details. ; Tanba no Shibamukuri {{Wikisource|Tanba no Shibamukuri}} The ritual is performed on October 10 at the Taku Shrine festival. Because the call during the festival is "chaa," the popular name for the ritual became "chaa." In 1816 ({{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka}} 13), it was called "chau"<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=伊崎義明 |title=峰山郷土芸能集 |publisher=峰山よし出版 |date=1971 |page= |isbn=}}</ref>.
Four theories have been handed down regarding the origin of the "{{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632|Shibamukuri}} ritual"<ref name=丹波9,6p/>. The first relates to prayers for victory during the {{jalink|Mongol|Q3875368}} invasions<ref name=丹波9,6p/>. The second holds that it is a ritual based on ancient precedent in the ''{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}'', mimicking the scene of young men leading the way and purifying the path of evil spirits with wasako bamboo ({{jalink|bamboo grass}})<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=子供がつづる丹後の歴史 |publisher=黒川美富子 |date=1980 |page=169 |isbn=}}</ref>. The third theory traces its origin to {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} {{jalink|dengaku|Q3022455}}, and the fourth attributes it to the "Sasa-bayashi" tradition found throughout the {{jalink|Tango region}}<ref name=丹波9,6p>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=会誌『旦波』第九号 |publisher=丹波の文化を伝承する会 |date=2016 |page=6 |isbn=}}</ref>.
=== Related sites === * {{jalink|Isanago-yama|Q31707112}} - A {{jalink|sacred mountain}} known for the Mount Hiji legend ({{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754}}) handed down in {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}}. It is said to be the site where "the celestial maiden of Hiji" descended, bringing rice cultivation and sake brewing to the {{jalink|Tango region}}<ref name="#1"/>.
=== Local information === === Location === * Address - {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyōtango|Q513897}}{{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}}{{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba||Tanba}} Wakutayama 2<ref name=アクセス/> * The shrine is located 1,262 meters northeast of {{jalink|Mineyama Station|Q6864579}} in a straight line, at an elevation of 29 meters above sea level<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mapion.co.jp/phonebook/M06005/26212/ILSP0061180050_ipclm/ |title=多久神社 |publisher =Mapion電話帳 |accessdate=2020-09-18}}</ref>. * Transportation access ** Railway - {{jalink|Kyoto Tango Railway}} "{{jalink|Mineyama Station|Q6864579}}" - 15 minutes on foot ** Bus - {{jalink|Tankai Bus}} "Tanba-guchi" bus stop - 3 minutes on foot; "Yada-bashi" bus stop - 6 minutes on foot <ref name=アクセス>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://yaokami.jp/1261189/ |title=多久神社アクセス |publisher =八百万の神 |accessdate=2020-09-21}}</ref>
=== Surroundings === * {{jalink|Wakutayama Tumulus Group|Q100549947}} - An extensive tumulus group numbering 42 mounds in total, located on the hills behind Taku Shrine<ref name=京丹後市の考古資料132p>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京丹後市の考古資料 |publisher=京丹後市 |date=2010 |page=132 |isbn=}}</ref>. * {{jalink|Kyōtango Municipal Tanba Elementary School|Q100594759|Former Tanba Elementary School}} - An elementary school located in the center of the {{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba}} district, founded in 1872 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 5) and closed in March 2018 (Heisei 30)<ref>「京丹後市 18年度、丹波と新山小を統合 19年度は豊栄と間人も」『毎日新聞』2016年8月30日</ref>. The students of this school performed the traditional ritual "{{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}}" at the annual grand festival<ref name=京都新聞19970509>{{Cite news |title=まちの文化財６ |newspaper=京都新聞 |date=1997-05-09 |author= |page=}}</ref>.
=== Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} <!-- === Annotations === {{notelist}} --> === Sources === {{reflist|2}}
=== References === * ''Mineyama Kyōdoshi, Volume 1'' (峰山郷土史 上, Mineyama Local History, Volume 1), Mineyama-chō, 1963 * ''Kyōtango-shi no Machinami, Kenchiku'' (京丹後市のまちなみ・建築, Streetscapes and Architecture of Kyōtango City), Kyōtango City, 2017 * Mineyama Board of Education, Mineyama-chō Cultural Properties Protection Council, ''Mineyama no Bunkazai (Shaji-hen)'' (みねやまの文化財(社寺編), Cultural Properties of Mineyama (Shrines and Temples Edition)), Mineyama Kōhansha, 1981 * ''Hozonban Maizuru, Miyazu, Tango no Konjaku'' (保存版 舞鶴・宮津・丹後の今昔, Preservation Edition: Maizuru, Miyazu, and Tango, Past and Present), Kyōdo Shuppansha, 2004 * ''Tango Shin Fudoki'' (丹後新風土記, New Tango Fudoki), Tango Wide-Area Tourism Campaign Council, 2008 * ''Kaishi "Tanba" Dai-9-gō'' (会誌『但波』第9号, Journal "Tanba" No. 9), Association for Transmitting the Culture of Tanba, 2016 * ''Kyōto Daijiten Fuiki-hen'' (京都大事典府域編, Great Encyclopedia of Kyoto, Prefectural Area Edition), {{jalink|Tankōsha|Q11559760}}, 1994 * ''Tango Mineyama Hagoromo Densetsu'' (丹後みねやま羽衣伝説, Tango Mineyama Hagoromo Legend), Kyoto Prefecture Mineyama-chō, 1998 * ''Tango Shiryō Sōsho'' (丹後史料叢書, Tango Historical Documents Collection), Volume 1, Tango Shiryō Sōsho Kankōkai, 1927. National Diet Library Digital Collection <nowiki>https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1175321</nowiki> (accessed 2025-06-18)

{{Infobox religious building | name = Taku Shrine | native_name = 多久神社 | image = Takushirine (4).jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Taku Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35.635451|135.079379}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }}
{{nihongo|'''Taku Shrine'''|多久神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Tango Province]].
== instance of (P31) ==

[[JA:多久神社]]
{{wikidata link|Q100551246}}
== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Taku Shrine |画像 = [[File:Takushirine (4).jpg|280px]] |所在地 = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyōtango|Q513897}}{{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba}} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|38|09.8|N|135|04|45.2|E|region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=多久神社}} |祭神 = Toyouganome no Mikoto ({{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Ōkami}}) |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, former {{jalink|district shrine|Q134917316}} |創建 = (traditionally) 39th year of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, 7th month, 7th day |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} with corner bracket |別名 = Tenshu Daimyōjin |札所等 = |例祭 = Weekend in mid-October |神事 = {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}} |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto|label=多久神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Taku Shrine''' (たくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|shrine|Q845945}} located in Ōaza Wakutayama, {{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba}}, {{jalink|Kyōtango|Q513897}}, {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}. Its main hall is registered as a {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}} {{jalink|tangible cultural property|Q2901860}} (building), and the {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632|Shibamukuri}} ritual performed at the autumn festival is registered as a Kyoto Prefecture {{jalink|intangible folk cultural property}}<ref name=郷土出版社>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=保存版 舞鶴・宮津・丹後の今昔 |publisher=郷土出版社 |date=2004 |page=55 |isbn=}}</ref>.
=== Enshrined deity === ; '''Toyouganome no Mikoto''' (とようがのめのみこと) : Also known as "{{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke Ōkami}}"<ref name=丹後新風土記/>. According to tradition, Toyouganome no Mikoto is identified with the celestial maiden ({{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754|Hagoromo celestial maiden}}) of {{jalink|Mount Hiji|Q11126130}} mentioned in the lost passages of the ''{{jalink|Tango no Kuni Fudoki|Q11368517}}'', who failed to return to heaven and settled in this area, cultivating rice and creating a sake that could cure all ailments<ref name=デジタルF10>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.kyotango.lg.jp/top/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/3/1/1/3405.html |title=デジタルミュージアムF10多久神社本殿 |publisher =京丹後市 |accessdate=2020-09-21}}</ref>. For this reason, until the {{jalink|Meiji}} era, the shrine was also called "Tenshu Daimyōjin" (Heavenly Sake Great Deity)<ref name=丹後新風土記>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=丹後新風土記 |publisher=丹後広域観光キャンペーン協議会 |date=2008 |page=239 |isbn=}}</ref>. : Furthermore, there is a legend that sake once gushed from the foot of {{jalink|Wakutayama Tumulus Group|Q100549947|Wakutayama}} hill<ref name=丹後新風土記/>, and a folk song has been passed down: "From the Tenshu-sama of Yada and Tanba village, even the water flowing below is worthy of reverence"<ref name="峰山郷土史上,460p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=460 |isbn=}}</ref>.
=== History === === Overview history === The shrine is identified as the small shrine "Taku no Jinja" among the nine shrines of {{jalink|Tanba District}} listed in the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}'' as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}<ref name="峰山郷土史454-455p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=454-455 |isbn=}}</ref>. According to tradition, the shrine was founded on the 7th day of the 7th month of the 39th year of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} at the site of a pond in {{jalink|Naka District (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11367236|Tanba Village}} where a {{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754|celestial maiden}} had descended<ref>{{Cite web |title=丹後史料叢書 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1175321/1/93?keyword=%E5%A4%9A%E4%B9%85%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2025-06-18 |publisher=国立国会図書館 |editor=丹後史料叢書刊行会 |year=1927 |language=ja |doi=10.11501/1175321}}</ref>. Before it was relocated to its current site during the {{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} era (1441-1444), it was located approximately 4,500 meters further to the west<ref name=京丹後デジタル/>.
The celestial maiden enshrined as the deity is identified with {{jalink|Toyouke Ōkami}}, and was revered as Tenshu Daimyōjin, the deity who brought sake brewing to this land. The founding site where the celestial maiden descended was a place called "Ota Pond" near where Taku Shrine stands in the early 21st century, and it is said that defiling it would bring a curse of heavy rain<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京都大事典府域編 |publisher=淡交社 |date=1994 |page=334 |isbn=}}</ref>. Notably, in the {{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754|Hagoromo tradition}} passed down in {{jalink|Mineyama}}, the pond where the celestial maiden descended is called "Manai" based on the lost passages of the ''{{jalink|Tango no Kuni Fudoki|Q11368517}}'' and the ''Tango Kyūjiki'', and the widely known theory holds that it is "Me-ike" (Female Pond) on {{jalink|Isanago-yama|Q31707112}}, which straddles {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}} and {{jalink|Ōmiya-chō (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11434439|Ōmiya-chō}}<ref name="#1">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=丹後みねやま羽衣伝説 |publisher=京都府峰山町 |date=1998年 |page=1 |isbn=}}</ref>
In documentary records, the ''Mineyama Meisaiki'' of 1753 (Hōreki 3) records that the shrine was "a six-shaku shrine dedicated to Tenshu Daimyōjin" and that "on the 8th of the 9th month, the miko Sagami of Kawabe Village was hired to perform the rites"<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=455 |isbn=}}</ref>.
The shrine served as the {{jalink|Tanba Village}} sōsha (head shrine) for the villages of Tanba, Naiki, Yada, Hashigi, Akasaka, Ishimaru, and Niiyama<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=会誌『旦波』第9号 |publisher=丹波の文化を伝承する会 |date=2016 |page=5 |isbn=}}</ref>. In 1873 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 6), it became the village shrine of {{jalink|Tanba Village|Q35797726}}<ref name="峰山郷土史上 459p"/>.
=== Taku Dispute === Around 1869 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 2), the Meiji government's policy of abolishing popular names and renaming shrines according to ancient {{jalink|Shinto|Q812767}} conventions triggered the "Taku Dispute" over the origins of '''Taku Shrine''' as the head shrine<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=457-458 |isbn=}}</ref>. A new theory was proposed: reading the two syllables of Tanba (丹波, ta-ni-ha) in reverse yields "ha-ni," and substituting the character for "two" (二) with "mi" gives "ha-mi," i.e., "Hami" (波弥), meaning the deity of food (hamu); therefore Tanba is the same as Hami, and the original site of Taku Shrine is a place in the neighboring villages of Arayama and Shinmachi called "Tako-dani" and "Tako-sengen," meaning the land of "Tako" -- that is, "Taku"<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p"/>. In response, Imanishi Iyo, who was then the custodian of Taku Shrine, petitioned the domain's shrine and temple office to clarify that {{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba||Tanba}} was unquestionably "Taniha" as recorded in ancient texts, and was emphatically not "Hami"<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p"/>.
The outcome of this dispute is not recorded in surviving documents, but according to the ''Jinja Torishirabechō'' (Shrine Investigation Record) submitted to the shrine and temple office, following the explanation of why Taku Shrine was relocated from its former site to its current location: "At that time (the early 1440s), Hashigi Village, Yada Village, Naiki Village, and Arayama Village had been conducting festival rites together, but due to a great dispute, the festival days became separate." This suggests that disputes with neighboring districts had existed for approximately 500 years prior<ref name="峰山郷土史 上,457-458p"/>.
=== Precincts === Behind the shrine stands the {{jalink|Wakutayama Tumulus Group|Q100549947}}, a group of 42 tumuli designated as a prefectural historic site, centering on a scallop-shaped {{jalink|keyhole-shaped burial mound|Q11268718}} approximately 100 meters in total length, along with various other large and small round tumuli. Behind the main hall, which is registered as a Kyoto Prefecture tangible cultural property and dates to the 17th century, stretches a forest of {{jalink|chinquapin|Q1065400}} and {{jalink|Mōsō bamboo|Q1330347}} trees. The {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632|Shibamukuri}} ritual folk performing art presented at the autumn festival is a prefecturally registered intangible folk cultural property that carries on the tradition of {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} fūryū kouta (elegant ballads), making this a region of great historical and cultural value<ref name=京丹後デジタル/>.
For this reason, on March 19, 2004 (Heisei 16), the main hall on the mountainside and approximately 1.7 {{jalink|hectares|Q35852}} of surrounding forest were designated as a prefectural Cultural Property Environmental Conservation District as "an area forming a valuable cultural landscape that should be preserved"<ref name=京丹後デジタル>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.kyotango.lg.jp/top/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/3/1/1/3458.html |title=多久神社文化財環境保全地区（府文化財環境保全地区） |publisher =京丹後市 |accessdate=2020-09-21}}</ref>.
=== Subsidiary shrines === In 1908 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 41), the following five shrines were merged as subsidiary shrines within the precincts<ref name=みねやまの文化財23p>{{Cite book|和書|author=峰山教育委員会 峰山町文化財保護審議会 |title=みねやまの文化財(社寺編) |publisher=峰山孔版社 |date=1981 |page=23 |isbn=}}</ref><ref name="峰山郷土史上,460p"/>. * Tatenui-sha - Enshrines Amawashi no Mikoto. * Yaegaki-sha - Enshrines Susanoo no Mikoto. * Hachiman-sha - Enshrines Homudawake no Mikoto. * Ōmiyagi-sha - Enshrines Ōmiyame no Mikoto. * Hiragaoka-sha - Enshrines Ukemochi no Ōkami. <gallery> File:多久神社 本殿正面.jpg|Worship hall File:多久神社 本殿内４.jpg|Main hall File:Takujinnja 社日の塔.jpg|Shajitsu-no-tō (shrine day tower) File:Takushirine (2).jpg|Torii gate and approach path File:Takushirine (3).jpg|Split worship hall-style building in front of the worship hall </gallery>
=== Shrine buildings === On September 25, 1811 ({{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka}} 8), the shrine suffered a fire that destroyed the main hall and its sheltering structure. The main hall that survives into the 21st century was rebuilt three years later using funds from the sale of five koku of shrine rice paddy land and donations from supporters<ref name=デジタルF10/>. The dance hall was also destroyed by fire in 1835 (Tenpō 6) and was rebuilt in 1877 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 10). The worship hall was newly constructed in 1912 ({{jalink|Taishō}} 1)<ref name="峰山郷土史上 459p"/>.
The shrine buildings surviving into the 21st century are as follows. ; Main hall <!--: It is believed to have been built between the mid-18th and early 19th centuries<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>。--><!--The explanation to the left contradicts the statement above that it was destroyed by fire in 1811.--> : The surviving main hall bears a {{jalink|giboshi ornament|Q3105399}} inscription dated Bunka 11, corresponding to 1814, and is believed to have been rebuilt by 1814 following the fire of 1811<ref name=デジタルF10/>. The building was the work of master carpenter Yoshioka Kahei, who was active in the {{jalink|Tango region}} at the time, and it survived without collapse even during the {{jalink|Kita-Tango earthquake|Q11400894}} of 1927 ({{jalink|Shōwa}} 2), which caused catastrophic damage throughout the {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}} area<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築/>. : It is a one-bay {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} main hall with corner brackets, a karahafu (cusped gable), and kokera-buki (wooden shingle) roofing, and is considered particularly valuable as a shrine main hall distinguished by its fine decorative carvings<ref name=デジタルF10/><ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築 |publisher=京丹後市 |date=2017 |page=465 |isbn=}}</ref>. A Kasuga-zukuri main hall is rare in the {{jalink|Tango region}}<ref name=丹後新風土記/>. : Although the main hall was severely damaged by the {{jalink|Kita-Tango earthquake|Q11400894}}, it was one of the few shrine buildings in {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}}, which suffered the greatest damage, to survive without collapse, and it was restored through approximately one month of repair work by the end of 1928 ({{jalink|Shōwa}} 3)<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築 |publisher=京丹後市 |date=2017 |page=272 |isbn=}}</ref><ref name="峰山郷土史上 459p">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=峰山郷土史 上 |publisher=峰山町 |date=1963 |page=459 |isbn=}}</ref>. ; Worship hall : An irimoya-zukuri (hipped gable) building, one bay in depth and two bays in width, with tsuma-iri (gable entry) and san-gawarabuki (pantile) roofing<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>. ; Sheltering structure : A kirizuma-zukuri (gable roof) building, four bays in depth and six bays in width, with tsuma-iri (gable entry) and steel sheet roofing<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>. ; Split worship hall-style building : A kirizuma-zukuri (gable roof) building, one bay in depth and one bay in width, with steel sheet roofing<ref name=京丹後市のまちなみ・建築,465p/>.
=== Festivals === The main festivals are as follows. * New Year * Autumn Festival - The annual grand festival held on a weekend in mid-October, at which the traditional ritual {{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}} is performed. However, in recent years, due to the declining birthrate, it is not held every year. * Minazuki Festival - Held on July 30 within the shrine precincts. Also called Kawasusosei (riverside festival)<ref name=みねやまの文化財23p/>.
=== Prefectural registered cultural properties === ; Main hall : See the "Shrine buildings" section for details. ; Tanba no Shibamukuri {{Wikisource|Tanba no Shibamukuri}} The ritual is performed on October 10 at the Taku Shrine festival. Because the call during the festival is "chaa," the popular name for the ritual became "chaa." In 1816 ({{jalink|Bunka era|Q1373146|Bunka}} 13), it was called "chau"<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=伊崎義明 |title=峰山郷土芸能集 |publisher=峰山よし出版 |date=1971 |page= |isbn=}}</ref>.
Four theories have been handed down regarding the origin of the "{{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632|Shibamukuri}} ritual"<ref name=丹波9,6p/>. The first relates to prayers for victory during the {{jalink|Mongol|Q3875368}} invasions<ref name=丹波9,6p/>. The second holds that it is a ritual based on ancient precedent in the ''{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}'', mimicking the scene of young men leading the way and purifying the path of evil spirits with wasako bamboo ({{jalink|bamboo grass}})<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=子供がつづる丹後の歴史 |publisher=黒川美富子 |date=1980 |page=169 |isbn=}}</ref>. The third theory traces its origin to {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845}} {{jalink|dengaku|Q3022455}}, and the fourth attributes it to the "Sasa-bayashi" tradition found throughout the {{jalink|Tango region}}<ref name=丹波9,6p>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=会誌『旦波』第九号 |publisher=丹波の文化を伝承する会 |date=2016 |page=6 |isbn=}}</ref>.
=== Related sites === * {{jalink|Isanago-yama|Q31707112}} - A {{jalink|sacred mountain}} known for the Mount Hiji legend ({{jalink|Hagoromo legend|Q1326754}}) handed down in {{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}}. It is said to be the site where "the celestial maiden of Hiji" descended, bringing rice cultivation and sake brewing to the {{jalink|Tango region}}<ref name="#1"/>.
=== Local information === === Location === * Address - {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyōtango|Q513897}}{{jalink|Mineyama|Q11476128}}{{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba||Tanba}} Wakutayama 2<ref name=アクセス/> * The shrine is located 1,262 meters northeast of {{jalink|Mineyama Station|Q6864579}} in a straight line, at an elevation of 29 meters above sea level<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.mapion.co.jp/phonebook/M06005/26212/ILSP0061180050_ipclm/ |title=多久神社 |publisher =Mapion電話帳 |accessdate=2020-09-18}}</ref>. * Transportation access ** Railway - {{jalink|Kyoto Tango Railway}} "{{jalink|Mineyama Station|Q6864579}}" - 15 minutes on foot ** Bus - {{jalink|Tankai Bus}} "Tanba-guchi" bus stop - 3 minutes on foot; "Yada-bashi" bus stop - 6 minutes on foot <ref name=アクセス>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://yaokami.jp/1261189/ |title=多久神社アクセス |publisher =八百万の神 |accessdate=2020-09-21}}</ref>
=== Surroundings === * {{jalink|Wakutayama Tumulus Group|Q100549947}} - An extensive tumulus group numbering 42 mounds in total, located on the hills behind Taku Shrine<ref name=京丹後市の考古資料132p>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=京丹後市の考古資料 |publisher=京丹後市 |date=2010 |page=132 |isbn=}}</ref>. * {{jalink|Kyōtango Municipal Tanba Elementary School|Q100594759|Former Tanba Elementary School}} - An elementary school located in the center of the {{jalink|Mineyama, Tanba}} district, founded in 1872 ({{jalink|Meiji}} 5) and closed in March 2018 (Heisei 30)<ref>「京丹後市 18年度、丹波と新山小を統合 19年度は豊栄と間人も」『毎日新聞』2016年8月30日</ref>. The students of this school performed the traditional ritual "{{jalink|Tanba no Shibamukuri|Q100550632}}" at the annual grand festival<ref name=京都新聞19970509>{{Cite news |title=まちの文化財６ |newspaper=京都新聞 |date=1997-05-09 |author= |page=}}</ref>.
=== Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} <!-- === Annotations === {{notelist}} --> === Sources === {{reflist|2}}
=== References === * ''Mineyama Kyōdoshi, Volume 1'' (峰山郷土史 上, Mineyama Local History, Volume 1), Mineyama-chō, 1963 * ''Kyōtango-shi no Machinami, Kenchiku'' (京丹後市のまちなみ・建築, Streetscapes and Architecture of Kyōtango City), Kyōtango City, 2017 * Mineyama Board of Education, Mineyama-chō Cultural Properties Protection Council, ''Mineyama no Bunkazai (Shaji-hen)'' (みねやまの文化財(社寺編), Cultural Properties of Mineyama (Shrines and Temples Edition)), Mineyama Kōhansha, 1981 * ''Hozonban Maizuru, Miyazu, Tango no Konjaku'' (保存版 舞鶴・宮津・丹後の今昔, Preservation Edition: Maizuru, Miyazu, and Tango, Past and Present), Kyōdo Shuppansha, 2004 * ''Tango Shin Fudoki'' (丹後新風土記, New Tango Fudoki), Tango Wide-Area Tourism Campaign Council, 2008 * ''Kaishi "Tanba" Dai-9-gō'' (会誌『但波』第9号, Journal "Tanba" No. 9), Association for Transmitting the Culture of Tanba, 2016 * ''Kyōto Daijiten Fuiki-hen'' (京都大事典府域編, Great Encyclopedia of Kyoto, Prefectural Area Edition), {{jalink|Tankōsha|Q11559760}}, 1994 * ''Tango Mineyama Hagoromo Densetsu'' (丹後みねやま羽衣伝説, Tango Mineyama Hagoromo Legend), Kyoto Prefecture Mineyama-chō, 1998 * ''Tango Shiryō Sōsho'' (丹後史料叢書, Tango Historical Documents Collection), Volume 1, Tango Shiryō Sōsho Kankōkai, 1927. National Diet Library Digital Collection <nowiki>https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1175321</nowiki> (accessed 2025-06-18)

{{Infobox religious building | name = Taku Shrine | native_name = 多久神社 | image = Takushirine (4).jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Taku Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35.635451|135.079379}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }}
{{nihongo|'''Taku Shrine'''|多久神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Tango Province]].
